sentences.base_language: Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?
sentences.target_language: すみません、駅にはどうやって行けばいいですか？
sentences.romanized: Sumimasen, eki ni wa dō yatte ikeba ii desu ka?
sentences.literal: Excuse-me, station how doing go if-good?
sentences.grammarNote: すみません (sumimasen) = 'excuse me / sorry to bother you' — the standard polite opener before asking a stranger for help. 〜ばいいですか is a natural way to ask 'how should I…?' or 'what's the best way to…?'

sentences.base_language: Go straight and then turn left.
sentences.target_language: まっすぐ行って、それから左に曲がってください。
sentences.romanized: Massugu itte, sorekara hidari ni magatte kudasai.
sentences.literal: Straight go, then left turn please.
sentences.grammarNote: The て-form (itte) links sequential actions: 'go straight and then…'. Direction words come before に: 左に曲がる (turn left), 右に曲がる (turn right). まっすぐ = 'straight ahead'.

sentences.base_language: The station is about ten minutes away.
sentences.target_language: 駅まで歩いて約十分です。
sentences.romanized: Eki made aruite yaku juppun desu.
sentences.literal: Station until walking about ten-minutes is.
sentences.grammarNote: まで (made) = 'until / up to / as far as': 駅まで (to the station / as far as the station). 約 (yaku) = 'approximately / about'. 歩いて (aruite) uses the te-form to mean 'by walking'.

sentences.base_language: Is there a bus stop nearby?
sentences.target_language: 近くにバス停はありますか？
sentences.romanized: Chikaku ni basutei wa arimasu ka?

sentences.base_language: Yes, it's right around the corner.
sentences.target_language: はい、すぐそこの角にあります。
sentences.romanized: Hai, sugu soko no kado ni arimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: すぐ (sugu) = 'right / immediately / very close'. すぐそこ = 'right there / very close by'. This adverb of proximity or immediacy is very useful for giving directions: すぐ前 (right in front), すぐそば (right nearby).

sentences.base_language: Which bus goes to the city center?
sentences.target_language: 市内中心部に行くバスはどれですか？
sentences.romanized: Shinai chūshinbu ni iku basu wa dore desu ka?
sentences.literal: City-center to going bus which is?
sentences.grammarNote: どれ (dore) = 'which one (of three or more)'. Compare: どれ (which one of many), どちら (which of two, also polite), どの + noun (which [noun]). 市内 (shinai) = 'in the city / urban area'.

sentences.target_language: ありがとうございました。

sentences.base_language: No problem, have a good trip!
sentences.target_language: どういたしまして、良い旅を！
sentences.grammarNote: 〜を (wo) at the end of a wish or toast implies 'may you have…': 良い旅を (have a good trip), 良い一日を (have a good day). The verb is omitted but understood — a common Japanese expression of goodwill.
